entry.title: Re: What does the 'Y' in 'YCOA' stand for? Something Chart of Accounts
entry.content:
Hi Dana,

The "Y" in YCOA is used as a prefix to differentiate between types of objects or to denote specific customizations. In SAP, the letters "Y" and "Z" are often used to indicate customer-specific or custom-developed objects, distinguishing them from standard SAP objects.

Also look up Why YCOA? The value of the standard Chart of Accou... - SAP Community and also Chart of Accounts in S/4 HANA Public Cloud (Cloud ... - SAP Community

entry.title: SAP's Pioneering Leap in Artificial Intelligence: Transforming Enterprise Processes with Business AI
entry.content:
SAP has significantly expanded its Artificial Intelligence (AI) footprint, integrating novel Business AI and Generative AI features across its product suite. These innovations are reshaping countless enterprise processes—from core finance operations to user experience—while delivering tangible benefits in time, effort, and cost efficiencies. As an SAP employee and passionate AI advocate, I'm eager to share how AI is now woven into the SAP fabric, the technical bedrock supporting these solutions, and the concrete business processes impacted by AI adoption.

1. The Evolution of SAP's AI Strategy

SAP's AI journey has grown from automating basic tasks to powering data-driven intelligence, predictive insights, and generative content across multiple business domains. In the last half-year, we've seen AI adoption accelerate under two complementary categories:

  1. Business AI: Purpose-built AI applications that address targeted enterprise challenges—like automated financial reconciliations, predictive demand management, or contract intelligence.
  2. Generative AI: Advanced models that generate new text, code, or visuals, fueling innovations in chatbot interactions, code suggestions, and automated content creation. The SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BT) is central to SAP's AI capabilities, standardizing data management, security, and lifecycle services. By providing a cohesive infrastructure, SAP BTP allows organizations to develop, deploy, and govern AI solutions in a scalable, secure manner.

2. Key AI Technologies and Deployment Methodologies

2.1 SAP AI Core and SAP AI Launchpad

Embedded in SAP BTP, SAP AI Core provides a robust execution layer for complex machine learning (ML) and deep learning workloads. It connects with SAP AI Launchpad, a central console for data scientists, ML engineers, and developers to orchestrate every stage of the ML lifecycle, including:

  • •Model Development: Create and refine ML models in Jupyter notebooks or other environments.
  • •Model Validation: Assess performance metrics before production.
  • •Deployment: Convert trained models into scalable microservices accessible via APIs.
  • •Monitoring: Track model performance and trigger retraining or decommissioning.

This integrated approach ensures that AI models can be deployed quickly and consistently across diverse SAP applications.

2.2 SAP Data Intelligence

SAP Data Intelligence streamlines large-scale data processing and AI workflows:

  • Data Integration: Connects various data sources (SAP S/4HANA, SAP HANA, external APIs) to unify and prepare data for AI projects.
  • ML Pipelines: This feature enables data scientists to design and run machine learning workflows using containerized microservices, from data preprocessing to model deployment.
  • Scalability: Built on Kubernetes, SAP Data Intelligence scales data pipelines according to workload demands.

Thanks to these features, enterprises can incorporate AI insights into real-time business processes—such as supply chain planning in SAP Integrated Business Planning (IBP) or forecasting in SAP Analytics Cloud—with minimal complexity.

2.3 SAP Intelligent Robotic Process Automation (SAP Intelligent RPA)

Repetitive manual tasks can often slow down enterprise operations, which is where SAP Intelligent RPA comes into play:

  • Automation: Configurable bots log into systems, fill fields, and trigger subsequent workflows across SAP and non-SAP environments.
  • AI-Enhanced Capabilities: Document recognition and computer vision allow bots to handle tasks like invoice capture, data validation, and purchase order entry accurately.
  • Central Orchestration: Bots are managed through a cloud-based interface on SAP BTP, ensuring quick deployment and monitoring at scale.

By relieving employees of routine tasks, SAP Intelligent RPA frees them up to tackle more strategic responsibilities.

2.4 SAP Conversational AI

Conversational interfaces dramatically improve user experience, and SAP Conversational AI is SAP's solution for building sophisticated chatbots:

  • Natural Language Processing (NLP): Advanced ML models parse user intent, understand context, and extract data from user conversations.
  • Training Interface: Bot developers can easily import training sentences, label entities, and build language models without deep coding.
  • Omnichannel Deployment: Once created, chatbots can be integrated into websites, social media, and mobile apps and connected to SAP S/4HANA or SAP CX.

The result is a self-service ecosystem where customers and employees get quick, accurate answers, even to complex queries.

3. Embedded AI Throughout SAP's Portfolio

SAP has rapidly extended AI features across its core applications and industry solutions. Notable examples include:

  1. SAP S/4HANA: Incorporates predictive analytics for financial close tasks, anomaly detection in invoices, and ML-based demand planning.
  2. SAP SuccessFactors: Includes AI-driven tools to analyze resumes, match candidates to job roles, and recommend upskilling options.
  3. SAP ISM (e.g. Ariba): Uses intelligent spend analysis, NLP-driven contract analysis, and supplier risk detection.
  4. SAP Fieldglass: Leverages AI for workforce planning, talent pool optimization and compliance checks.
  5. SAP Analytics Cloud: Offers predictive scenario generation and conversational analytics to transform dashboards into proactive insight hubs.
  6. SAP Integrated Business Planning (IBP): Embeds AI-based forecasting incorporating internal and external variables—like trends, social sentiment, or climate data—for more accurate planning.

Across all these systems, SAP AI Core, SAP AI Launchpad, and SAP Data Intelligence provide the foundation to ensure seamless AI adoption, robust lifecycle management, and high scalability.

4. Generative AI Use Cases: A Catalyst for Enterprise Innovation

Generative AI is emerging as a powerful tool within the SAP environment, pushing the boundaries of how organizations organize, create and optimize optimal workflows. Recent enhancements revolve around:

  1. Code Generation
    • Generative models help developers by recommending code snippets and configurations tailored to SAP's ABAP environment or other programming languages. This feature speeds development cycles and reduces errors, as the AI model references large repositories of best practices.
  2. Document Summaries
    • Leveraging large language models (LLMs), AI can read lengthy HR policies, contractual documents, or financial statements and produce concise summaries. This helps employees and managers quickly identify key insights without sifting through pages of text.
  3. Content Creation
    • SAP Marketing Cloud users can benefit from AI-driven suggestions for ad copy, mail subject lines, and campaign slogans. Marketers can refine AI-generated text to maintain brand voice, achieving faster marketing time with more engaging content.
  4. Enhanced Chatbots
    • Pairing SAP Conversational AI with generative language models can produce more natural, context-rich responses. This goes beyond simple Q&A bots and delivers a more human-like dialogue experience, improving user satisfaction and employee efficiency.

5. AI-Driven Processes for Enterprise Transformation

Rather than simply citing numbers, seeing how these AI enhancements impact day-to-day enterprise processes is essential. Below are specific workflows illustrating how AI brings tangible benefits in time, effort, and overall operational excellence.

5.1 Financial Invoice Processing

  1. In finance departments, invoice management often involves manual data extraction, validation, and entry into SAP S/4HANA. By combining SAP Intelligent RPA bots with machine learning-based invoice recognition, enterprises can:
    1. Automate Data Extraction: AI reads invoice fields such as supplier name, amount, tax, and dates.
    2. Validate Against Master Data: To flag mismatches, the system compares extracted details to existing vendor records or purchase orders.
    3. Initiate Approvals: Once verified, the invoice automatically moves into the approval workflow in SAP S/4HANA.
  • This end-to-end automation minimizes manual effort, reduces billing errors, and ensures more accurate financial statements.

5.2 Procurement Compliance

  • In procurement, contract management and supplier onboarding can be incredibly time-consuming. SAP Ariba, augmented by NLP and A, I can streamline these steps:
    • Contract Analysis: AI reads contract clauses to identify compliance issues or potential risks, eliminating the need for line-by-line manual review.
    • Supplier Performance Insights: AI synthesizes from multiple touchpoints—like on-time delivery and quality records—to score vendor reliability.
    • Automatic Alerts: If anomalies or mismatches occur between a supplier's invoice and contract terms, the system highlights them for further action.
    • This higher-level visibility ensures procurement teams can focus on negotiating better deals and strengthening supplier relationships rather than being bogged down by mundane checks.

5.3 Customer Support and Self-Service

  • Customer experience is paramount in every industry. AI-infused chatbots in SAP Conversational AI can enhance service processes:
    1. 24/7 Query Handling: Chatbots tackle repetitive requests (e.g., order status, password resets) at any hour.
    2. Tiered Escalation: More complex issues route to human agents, enriched by AI-summarised text so agents can respond faster.
    3. Process Integration: The chatbot triggers workflows in SAP S/4HANA, SAP CX, or other systems for tasks like order cancellations or status updates.
  • Over time, these bots can learn from fundamental interactions, refine answers, and adapt to frequently asked questions to reduce response times.

5.4 Workforce and Talent Management

  • Leveraging AI in HR processes improves talent acquisition, development, and engagement within SAP SuccessFactors:
    1. Resume Screening: Machine learning algorithms match candidate profiles to job requirements, cutting out manual resume filtering.
    2. Skill Gap Detection: The system identifies skills that existing employees need and recommends learning paths in SAP SuccessFactors Learning.
    3. Predictive Retention: AI examines employee turnover patterns, detecting early signs of disengagement or burnout so HR teams can take proactive measures.
  • HR leaders gain a deeper understanding of workforce dynamics through these processes and can quickly implement targeted interventions.

5.5 Supply Chain Forecasting and Planning

  • Supply chains often face volatile market conditions and fluctuating consumer demands. SAP IBP, backed by AI-based forecasting, transforms planning processes:
    1. Data Enrichment: Machine learning models incorporate external signals (e.g., economic indicators and weather forecasts) to improve demand planning.
    2. Inventory Optimization recommends safety stock adjustments based on real-time demand patterns, ensuring products are stocked where and when needed.
    3. Scenario Simulations: Planners run "what-if" simulations to gauge the impact of changing lead times or supplier constraints, all powered by predictive analytics.
  • This approach shortens planning cycles and helps mitigate the risk of overstocking or understocking vital goods.

6. Ensuring Responsible AI Deployment

As AI infiltrates more aspects of enterprise operations, SAP places a strong emphasis on responsible AI:

  1. Data Privacy and Security: Strict governance policies on SAP BTP ensure encryption, anonymization and role-based access.
  2. Ethical Framework: SAP's AI ethics guidelines promote fairness, transparency, and accountability, with regular checks for unintended bias in training data.
  3. Lifecycle Governance: By centralizing the SAP AI Core, companies can monitor model drift, manage versioning, and retrain or retire AI services as needed.

Responsible AI is pivotal in maintaining stakeholder trust and ensuring the long-term viability of AI-driven strategies.

7. What's Next for SAP AI?

We anticipate deeper AI integrations across SAP S/4HANA and other SAP Cloud solutions. Generative AI will extend beyond text and code generation into new domains, such as:

  1. Blueprinting for SAP Solution Manager: Automatically drafting solution documentation.
  2. Image and Video Analysis: AI could identify defects in real-time camera feeds in manufacturing contexts or quality inspections.
  3. Fiori Applications: Embedding multi-modal AI elements—like voice-based commands or augmented reality overlays—within SAP Fiori user interfaces.

SAP's continued collaboration with hyperscalers (e.g., Microsoft Azure, AWS, Google Cloud) and open-source AI communities ensures a steady influx of new capabilities and governance improvements. Simultaneously, expanding low-code/no-code AI development tools will empower more employees to build AI solutions without extensive coding backgrounds.

8. Conclusion

SAP's infusion of AI—spanning Business AI and Generative AI—has rapidly transformed how enterprises can optimize everyday workflows. Through solutions like SAP AI Core, SAP AI Launchpad, SAP Data Intelligence, SAP Intelligent RPA, and SAP Conversational AI, organizations have a holistic AI platform that is reliable, scalable, and secure.

By embedding AI into significant products such as SAP S/4HANA, SAP HXM (SuccessFactors), SAP ISM, SAP Fieldglass, SAP Analytics Cloud, and SAP IBP, SAP has systematically integrated machine learning into finance, procurement, HR, customer support, and supply chain processes. This focus on process transformation—rather than mere numeric gains—demonstrates how AI can accurately handle complex tasks, freeing up human talent for more strategic and creative pursuits.

With each quarter, SAP's AI roadmap continues evolving, delivering enhanced automation, predictive analytics, and intelligent user experiences. As we look to the future, expect even more powerful AI-driven capabilities, deeper business process insights, and broader adoption across industries. This is just the beginning of what AI can achieve within the SAP ecosystem, and enterprises that embrace these innovations now stand to become the Intelligent Enterprises of tomorrow.

#SLIThoughtLeadership

entry.title: Re: Managed RAP Remote Service Based CAPM App: Gateway HT...
entry.content:
I’ve identified the root cause of the batch failure. The POST BATCH request (which consists of a GET call) expects the following header on the outer HTTP request:

Accept:multipart/mixed

Now, I need to ensure that these headers are applied only to batch requests and not to regular GET requests.

I’ve attempted to set this condition in my service.js file, but the implementation is only setting Content-Type to multipart/mixed, not the Accept header.

Service.js:

 

const cds = require('@sap/cds') // Importing the CDS framework

// Export the service implementation
module.exports = cds.service.impl(async function () {

    const remotesrv = await cds.connect.to('<remote service>');
    this.on('READ', 'remotesrv', async req => {
        try {

            // Determine if the request is a BATCH request
            const isBatchRequest = req.path.includes('/$batch') || req.path.length > 800;

            // Prepare headers dynamically
            const headers = {
                'Accept': isBatchRequest ? 'multipart/mixed' : 'application/json',
                'Accept-Encoding': 'gzip, deflate',
                'x-api-key': process.env.APIKEY,
            };

            // Send the request to the external service
            return await remotesrv.send({
                query: req.query,
                headers,
            });

        } catch (error) {
            console.error('Error while sending request:', error);
            req.error(500, 'Error during external service call');
            console.log('main service query', req.query);
        }
    });

 

Is there a better approach to reliably differentiate batch requests and ensure the correct headers are applied? anything at the package.json setting for remote service ?

entry.title: Re: PaPM Universal Model: Parameter
entry.content:
Hi Rohit,

Currently in Universal Model, as a process user, we only have parameter option in Activity level. In your case, you can use the Transfer Parameters option in Manage Process when you are configuring the Activity Link. Using this option, it will transfer a parameter value from the activity to the succeeding activity or process step.

Just an additional information regarding Parameters, Parameters can be also used in Complex Selection of a Join and in Formula of a Calculation. You may refer to the blogpost my colleague published which is on this link https://community.sap.com/t5/financial-management-blogs-by-sap/sap-papm-cloud-universal-model-parameter-usage/ba-p/13981072

Thanks and Best Regards,
Samantha

entry.title: Configure Journal Entry Posting Approval from Store Template in Build Process Automation
entry.content:
Step1

Prerequisites

The following SAP Business Technology Platform services are required to consume the content package Journal Entry Posting Approval:

 

  • SAP Build Process Automation to orchestrate the process.
  • SAP Cloud Identity Services - Identity Authentication
  • SAP S/4HANA, private edition
  • SAP Business Technology Platform, Cloud Foundry runtime
  • SAP Work Zone, standard or advanced edition for accessing My Inbox and Process Visibility workspaces.
  • SAP Business Application Studio for SAP UI5 application 
  • SAP Build Process Automation instance
  • SAP Document Management Service
  • SAP Desktop Agent 3.19 or higher – need to install in desktop or laptop.

 

Step 2

Destination Configuration

  1. Setup S/4 HANA - S4HANA_JOURNALENTRY_MG4

Pointing to S4 HANA on-premises system. And it should be configured in cloud connector. You have to use virtual host and port configured in cloud connector in URL.

 

Name

S4HANA_JOURNALENTRY_MG4

Type

HTTP

Proxy Type

On-Premise

User

<Onpremise-User>

Password

<Onpremise-Password>

Authentication

Basic Authentication

URL

<URL configured in Cloud connector for S4 HANA>

Additional Properties

 

HTML5.DynamicDestination: true,

sap-client:<S4 Client>,

sap-platform: abap,

sap.applicationdevelopment.actions.enabled: true,

sap.processautomation.enabled: true,

WebIDEEnabled: true,

WebIDEUsage: odata_abap,dev_abap

Once created destination, check connection. You should get below success message

This template uses APIs and communication scenarios & these APIs are needed to be configured in SAP S/4HANA. The detail of these APIs  and required roles , cloud connector configuration you can refer configuration guide given in store from Page No 7 to 15.

  1. Document Management

The Input Template document for Sales Contract and any supporting documents can be uploaded in the approval form which are then made available as attachments for review and approval to the respective participants. For this you will need document management service and a corresponding destination in SAP BTP to connect to repository . But this step is optional.   

Create Instance for Document Management Service, under Instances and subscriptions. 

Go to service instances and search for Document Management Service, Integration Option. Click instances of DMS. You will see instance details as below

Under Service Keys, click three dots which will show popup with View option. Click View.

Copy highlighted values from service key as per below screenshot

Please follow below help link for further information

https://help.sap.com/docs/build-process-automation/sap-build-process-automation/create-repository-in-sap-document-management-service

Once subscribed to DMS, create destination named "sap_process_automation_document_store" and bind the respective values for client id, client secret & URL from document management service instance. 

Name

sap_process_automation_document_store

Type

HTTP

URL

Endpoints.ecmservice.url

Proxy Type

Internet

Authentication

Oauth2ClientCredentials

Client ID

uaa.clientid

Client Secret

uaa.clientsecret

Token Service URL

uaa.url + /oauth/token

3. SAP BPA Destination

sap_process_automation_service

If you are already having build process automation subscription, by default sap_process_automation_service destination will be available under destintation. Otherwise you can create by following below steps.

From BTP Cockpit select Instances and Subscription . Search for SAP Build Process Automation.

Select instance.

Click three dots in instance key -> Select View

Create destination by using service key details highlighted in screenshot. Respective path also given below.

 

Name

sap_process_automation_service

Type

HTTP

URL

Endpoint.api + /workflow/rest/v1

 

Proxy Type

Internet

Authentication

Oauth2ClientCredentials

ClientID

uaa.clientid

Client Secret

uaa.clientsecret

Token Service URL

 

Additional Properties

·         AppgyverEnabled: true

·         Endpoints: { "api":   <endpoints.api>}

·         html5-apps-repo:  {         "app_host_id": <html5-apps-repo.app_host_id>}

·         HTML5.DynamicDestination: true

·         Saasregistryenabled: true

·         sap.cloud.service: com.sap.spa.processautomation

·         sap.cloud.service.alias: spa

·         sap.processautomation.enabled: true

·         WebIDEEnabled: true

Remember while copying html5-apps-repo copy entire value starting from{.

Ex :  { “hostid” , “host id}

Similar way for endpoints copy entire value  {

Ex: {         "api":  <endpoint>     }

And then check connection. You will see below message.

4. SAP Build Process Automation Email

Configure email destination which will be used to send email to the approver, requestor, and admin at different staged of the process execution

Follow the steps to Configure a SMTP Mail Destination in your BTP account.

https://help.sap.com/docs/build-process-automation/sap-build-process-automation/configuring-smtp-mail-destination

Step 3

 

Configure APIs in SAP S/4HANA

This template uses the following APIs and communication scenarios & these APIs are needed to be configured in SAP S/4HANA. The detail of these APIs and required roles , cloud connector configuration you can refer configuration guide given in store from Page No 7 to 15

Step 4

Create Project from template in BPA

 

Goto Build Process Automation Cockpit, in left side pane, you can see Store

Click on Store - > and Enter Journal Entry Posting in search bar. Select first project in which publisher is SAP Build Process Automation.

You will navigate to this below page, where description of project, artifacts involved, configuration guide, required dependencies detailed out

You can download configuration guide by clicking pdf icon under Documents section

Now choose Create from Template option in top right corner of the page. You will get below Popup. Give your project name and click create.

Now from lobby you can see project got created. Click on project name.

You will be taken to overview page

Click icon nearby Overview. And you get to see artifacts overview in the below format.

Step 5

Deploy Project

Once imported, release and deploy from the Lobby. Before deployment, have to do some changes in determine approver step to change approver email id of workflow.

Configuration guide has brief steps from page 16 to 18. You can follow the same steps.

In journal Entry posting, action group created based on SAP SNC (Single Sign On) and SAP basic connection.

Either one of them required to run application. If you are using cloud connector configuration SNC is not required in this case.

Document Check – Using SAP basic connection

Document Check (SNC) – Using Secure Network Communication

Document Post – Using SAP basic connection

Document Post(SNC) - Using Secure Network Communication

In workflow, SNC based action group is used. Have to change it basic connection based action group.

Replace existing SNC action group in (Journal Entry Document Check) automation with Document Check

For that, from artifacts select Journal Entry Document Check highlighted as below

In the below screen, remove action group BAPI DOCUMENT_CHECK (SNC). Drag and drop BAPI_ACC_DOCUMENT_CHECK under Document Check as below.

Fill in required parameters in right pane.

Parameters:

Connection Type

Basic

Message Server

<Backend System server IP>

Instance Number

<Backend System Instance No>

Client

<Backend System Client>

User

< Backend System login user>

Password

< Backend System login password>

Next step is bind the value for BAPIResponse. Once replaced SNC with Document Check, will get error in some of steps. Let’s resolve.  Click step Set Variable: BAPI Response. Select edit icon highlighted below in screenshot

Edit expression as per below screenshot

Similar way change set Variable document Error by following screenshots.

Go to Overview page of Journal Entry Posting and select Journal Entry Document Post automation as highlighted below.

Delete current action group in step 5 and drag and drop BAPI_ACC_DOCUMENT_POST in same step.

Select step 5. And fill details like message server (s4 hana system IP address), instance number, client, username and password

Once changed document post, will get some errors in other steps. Follow below steps to fix errors. Click step no 8 and in right pane, select highlighted icon to edit value

Change value as per below screenshot

Select step 9. And edit value from right pane.

Change expression as per below screenshot and save.

Journal Document Entry Post Automation:

Post completing all the changes, process needs to be released and deployed from SAP Build to reflect the changes.

While deploying you will get below popup, choose destination which is pointing to cloud connector URL of S4 HANA System.

Step 6

Deploy UI5 applications into BTP Cockpit

From artifacts, we have to deploy ui5 application into BTP cockpit from Business Application Studio.

Click Journal-Entry-Posting-JournalEntryApproval_ui.tar. Click Download button in below screen. Similarly download Journal-Entry-Posting-JournalEntryApproval_ui.tar as well.

Go to Business Application Studio. Extract downloaded mtar file. From BAS Click File-> Import Project -> select extracted project zip file.

Open mta.yaml file and change SBPA-instance to your build process automation instance name . You can find instance name under instances in BTP cockpit  as below.

 

SAP Build Process Automation as the successor product of SAP Workflow Management has Workflow, Business Rules and Process Visibility capabilities of Workflow Management

In this, we are going to use Build process automation capabilities not workflow management.So we have to replace bpmworkflowruntime destination with BPA destination.

Go to your xs-app.json.  change 1st route pointing to bpmworkflowruntime with below

{

       "source": "^/sap_process_automation_service/(.*)$",

        "target": "$1",

        "destination": "sap_process_automation_service",

        "authenticationType": "xsuaa",

        "csrfProtection": false

    },

Once  replaced,  your xs-app.json will look like below .

Destination S4HANA_JOURNALENTRY_MG4 which we have created earlier in cockpit for S4 HANA on-premise system.

 sap_process_automation_service is destination which will be created by default while you are running booster for build process automation.Go to CreateView.Controller.

Existing

In getWorkflowRuntimeBaseURL change url as below

URL in destination has path till v1<bpa endpoint>/workflow/rest/v1

So just replace /bpmworkflowruntime/public/workflow/rest/v1

Go to startWorkflowInstance function and replace definitionId with yours. You can get definition id from below path SAP BPA->Monitoring - >Processes and Workflows .

This definitionId will be available once you have deployed project .

Comment out header section. Token fetch call is configured in destination level. So this section not required in our case.

Once completed all the above changes deploy application into Cockpit.

 

Another mtar file named Journal-Entry-Posting-JournalEntryApproval_ui.tar. Extract it and import zip file into BAS. Then deploy. No changes required in approval level.  In case you need additional information add it and deploy.

 

 

 

Step 7

Agent Registration

Before running application, go to desktop agent installed in your local machine. Select Settings -> mode and activate Unattended mode. Because Document Check and Document Post are unattended automation which will be triggered in background once we submitted journal entry from UI5 application.

Next step have to add our agent to agent management. Go to build process automation lobby. Choose Control Tower then Agents under Agent Configuration.

Select Register Agent

After registration, you can see your laptop hostname under Items.

For further details on this , please follow this link https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_BUSINESS_ONE/68a2e87fb29941b5bf959a184d9c6727/972ce532e66d48d8a7b43236fbb50248.html

Step 8

Testing Journal Entry Posting

Good to go. Now we can run application directly from deployed url in BTP -> HTML5 applications.

If you want to configure Journal Entry ui5 application in Build work zone standard edition, please follow the steps in this blog

https://developers.sap.com/tutorials/integrate-with-work-zone..html

To enable My Inbox, Process Workspace, Monitor Workflows, follow the steps in this blog

https://developers.sap.com/tutorials/spa-configure-workzone.html

 

Go to BTP Cockpit. Choose HTML5 applications. Search for Journal

Click JournalEntryStartUimodule. Once application loaded, you could see below screen.

You have two options to post journal entry. Either manually enter data in form or upload excel. Excel template you can find in Project artifacts

Choose Upload in Posting Via drop down. Then upload file and submit.

Once submitted, from build process automation lobby, go to monitoring and select highlighted icon as shown in pic

My Inbox application will open in another tab.

In My Inbox, you can see new task. Enter required comments  and Approve the task.

Incase any issues, you didn’t get any task in My Inbox, from lobby go to monitoring -> Click Process and Workflow Instances.

In instances filter definition choose journal entry posting approval process , instance filter status select all . Tasks will be listed down. Choose task and see flow of execution and where it got failed

Step 9

Check journal entries from Fiori Launchpad

Open SAP Fiori launchpad from backend system. From launchpad select highlighted tile. And you can see journal entry posted from our end. Successfully we have configured Journal Entry .

Successfully configured journal entry posting from bot store.

entry.title: Re: Inventory should be blocked at the delivery and not at picking
entry.content:
Hi Andreas,

Currently, during the Batch assignment in VL10i, the system may select a Batch that is already blocked by a delivery with a pending picking process. This causes an error when processing the VL06o and, in some cases, when executing the LT03, resulting in the message: "System cannot find source storage bin."

The VL10i transaction does not properly verify if the assigned Batch is blocked by another delivery with an unfinished picking process. This leads to operational conflicts and errors during the picking and stock transfer steps.

I need to remove this block at batch level. It is possible to do it by a change at the ATP?

Thanks

entry.title: Re: SAP BW/4HANA: The Grey Panther of Data Warehousing
entry.content:
Hi Phil!
thanks for your contribution. In our ecosystem, there are many SMEs with an experience like us working on different BW releases, in fact many of us took to full evolution from BW 3.x to 7.x to BWonHANA and BW/4HANA finally. But we need to be open-minded regarding SAP Datasphere as well, as it provides a new way of data warehousing.

You are right in highlighting some recent core enhancements which are related proven BW concepts. SAP Datasphere will evolve further in high pace and sooner or later all BW customer will face the decision which way to go in future. SAP puts a high focus on SAP Datasphere and related components. On Thursday,  February 13th, 5-6pm CET there will be a an important announcement about future evolution of SAP Datasphere. All of us should attend - please register and attend!!
https://www.sap.com/events/business-unleashed.html

Regards to Madrid! Frank

entry.title: Re: Why I am not able to see the Branch Section in my ABA...
entry.content:
There's no branch concept in "offline repository", the concept is to store the files in a ZIP file, and not the .git folder containing the whole git stuff like branches and so on.

If you don't want to get a git repository in the Web (github and so on), you may define https to your own ABAP system by installing larshp/abapGitServer: Git server implemented in ABAP.

entry.title: Re: SAP BW/4HANA: The Grey Panther of Data Warehousing
entry.content:
@Phil_from_Madrid- Spon on!

@MKreitlein - I thought I was the only one who is comfortable with "loop and read table" but now, I see that there are people who find SQL more complicated than ABAP.

1. I wonder why people do not give importance to SID and DIM ID concept of BW which I feel was ahead of its time. Because in any ETL tool, you always had to make this association/join but SAP BW used to do it for you which Datasphere lacks. So, in that sense, Datasphere is more of a demotion from what BW used to be.

2. You cannot import a data to .csv/flat file from Datasphere table which was a quite easy task in BW.

3. To place a file via process chain on CFTP/SFTP server with the use of std. FM is a quite straight forward thing, but it is quite a task or almost impossible in Datasphere.

4. Concept of openhub destination.

5. Broadcasting/scheduling functionality either via BW or from BO is missing in DSP/SAC. You cannot broadcast/schedule live datasphere data via SAC to user's e-mail ID in .xlsx or .pdf format.

6. Let's not even discuss the transport of objects in Datasphere. In my first implementation, I realized that the source system name should be abstract and same something like SWX where X stands for D, Q or P.

entry.title: Re: Migration to SAP DS 4.3
entry.content:
@Sharvari1 

I am not sure what repository wise migration means , but i will assume you are referring to a scenario where you build a new environment and do the upgrade there giving you some kind of a Zero Downtime deployment.
1. Create a new database schema
2. Use the DS 4.2 Repository Manager to create a ds 4.2 repository in the new DB schema.
3. Use al_engine cli or designer to export the repository objects from the old DS 4.2 repository to an ATL file
4. Use al_engine cli or designer to import the ATL file to the new DS 4.2 repository
5. Use the new DS 4.3 Repository to upgrade the new DS 4.2 repositories to 4.3 version.
6. Use Promotion Management to migrate CMS repository objects. The list of Objects to be promoted can be found here https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_DATA_SERVICES/5372c7960111477e9dea8931e087870c/2a3feccf1e014fd0ba9b829a06822a11.html?locale=en-US
7. Modify the repositories in CMC to ensure they point to the new 4.3 repositories
7. Register the new repositories with the Job Server
8. End-to-End testing
9. Decommission the old environment.

 

Scenario: Migrate to a new environment

Regards Joe

entry.title: Re: Highlights of the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2502 Release
entry.content:
Hello @MustafaBensan 

Nice to meet you! I am Harish from the Product Success Organization at SAP, and I’m here to help answer your question.

 For SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud, the current approach for partner add-on development remains valid. A clean core strategy is crucial for ensuring stable operations while fostering agility and innovation. Partners can utilize in-app (on-stack) or side-by-side extensibility and create extensions with the SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP), using tools like SAP Cloud Platform Integration and SAP API Business Hub to develop decoupled extensions. This approach maintains upgrade stability and compatibility.

 SAP has a certification process that ensures partner add-ons are compatible with S/4HANA environments. Partners should submit their add-on to the SAP Integration and Certification Center (ICC). The ICC tests and certifies that the add-on integrates seamlessly with SAP systems and meets all technical requirements.

 For more details please check this:

  • Add-on deployment Certification for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition
  • https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_ADD-ON_ASSEMBLY_KIT
  • https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blogs-by-sap/certification-of-partner-solutions-following-clean-core/ba-p/13556247
  • https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blogs-by-sap/list-of-certified-abap-add-ons-for-sap-s-4hana-cloud-private-edition/ba-p/13513201

 Let me know if you have any further questions!

 Thank you.

entry.title: Re: Why I am not able to see the Branch Section in my ABA...
entry.content: It's completely different. In abapGit, "offline repository" means a list of files containing only the ABAP objects, there's no GIT concept inside this ZIP file (so, no branch). A git repository stores the ABAP objects as "git objects" which can be of type "commit", "blob" and so on, which contain the information of branches, ABAP objects, etc.

entry.title: Re: SAP BW/4HANA: The Grey Panther of Data Warehousing
entry.content:
@FrankRiesner : Fully agree with "the open mind". In my experience, the development cycles are noticeably shorter, also because of the drag&drop for view development (other might call it low-code), but also because I feel very comfortable with expressing functionality in relational logic (aka SQL) which is now omnipresent in Datasphere (and which is very natural way of working for structured data). My point was that BW solved already plenty of aspects which are now being discussed in other products), which is also fine because Rome was not built on a single day. 

@MKreitlein & @Mandar_Damle I agree with you that DS is not (yet) that advanced as BW when it comes to develop bespoke functionality around it. The ABAP stack was a fully blown app server which is still used under S/4(!) and which is very powerful for certain tasks.

SID generation is however something owned to the past when CPU and physical data storage on HDD via Classic DB were the driving technologies. I don't miss SIDs much, even if a JOIN on INTEGER (SID) will be in most cases faster than a JOIN on a e.g. String (External key).

It's is true that defining an IOBJ was a fine functionality because of its semantic clarity, but we are approaching it step by step with the semantics of tables and views. Re the Loop at itab: This was also the way we needed to implement transformation logic in ABAP TRFNs, but many times, what we finally did was expressing nothing else than e.g. a simple JOIN in relational logic. It is so much easier to express this in DS, that's why I wrote to Frank above that it seems to me that dev cycles is DS are shorter than in BW, many things are more straight-forward. But it is true that DS also requests a deeper understanding about SQL than clasic ABAP OpenSQL (which is very limited compared to "normal" SQL) and a good knowledge of the function library. If you dominate SQL, you dominate big  parts of DS and any other DWH solution becomes easily approachable.

entry.title: How to post a new blog in SAP CRM & CX ?
entry.content:

entry.title: Re: Signavio Process Insights: Flexible WF PO
entry.content:
Hello @GNunnari,

Thank you for reaching out with your inquiry regarding PO approvals in Flexible Workflow and how they are reflected in Signavio Process Insights.

Currently, our logic for approval-related KPPURCH2 flows* considers a combination of EKKO-FRGGR (Release Group) and EKKO-PROCSTAT (Processing State). However, since Flexible Workflows do not populate the EKKO-FRGGR field, these cases are not currently reflected in the context boxes. Only approval cases with a release strategy are captured.

Our team is working on the corrections. In order to stay updated, I would recommend you to open a ticket via SAP for Me under the component BPI-PI-PPI. Here is a step-by-step guideline on how to create a ticket.

Please feel free to share your ticket number in a private message so I can forward it to our responsible colleagues.

Thank you and kind regards

Ascha
(virtual Customer Success Team at SAP Signavio)

entry.title: Re: Migration to SAP DS 4.3
entry.content:
Hi @jmuiruri , thank you for the response!

Yes , We are looking to reduce downtime of our the environment.

I will explain repo-wise approach a bit :

Say we have 10 repositories in SAP DS 4.2 env.

During migration, we will migrate 6 repositories to SAP DS 4.3 env and keep the remaining 4 to the existing (SAP DS 4.2) environment. This will ensure that the jobs in the repositories that run in the 4.2 environment run and only the jobs belonging to the 6 repositories will be down during the migration. Once we are sure that the migrated jobs run in the SAP DS 4.3 environment, we will migrate the remaining repositories to the SAP DS environment. Please let us know if we can do this kind of migration of repositories (in chunks)?

Also, do you recommend migrating using ATL files or using Promotion Management since we have 1000 jobs per repository?

Any insights will be helpful !

Regards,

Sharvari

entry.title: Create PostgreSQL (Hyperscaler Option - Cloud) on SAP BTP and connect it to SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
Introduction

I found some good guides and explainations to connect a PostgreSQL (On-Premise) with SAP Integration Suite. But what about PostgreSQL (Cloud) on SAP BTP? 
Many customers have a SAP BTP in place and they don't want to build up a database in their own network or in another cloud provider like Azure or AWS. So, here is my first guide to setup a PostgreSQL (Hyperscaler Option) on SAP BTP and connect it to SAP Integration Suite.

Important Links
https://help.sap.com/docs/postgresql-hyperscaler-option/postgresql-on-sap-btp-hyperscaler-option/what-is-postgresql-hyperscaler-option
https://help.sap.com/docs/postgresql-hyperscaler-option/postgresql-on-sap-btp-hyperscaler-option/service-plans-and-entitlements

Content (Steps)

1. Setup the PostgreSQL db on SAP BTP 
2. JDBC Configuration in SAP Integration Suite
3. Connect to PostgreSQL with (local) DBeaver
4. Create IFlow to push data into the db

 

1. Setup the PostgreSQL db on SAP BTP 

1.1 Log in to you SAP BTP Cockpit and go to the service marketplace of your subaccount

1.2 Search for "PostgreSQL"


1.3 Create a new instance with service plan "standard"
Note: service plan "free" is not possible here because there you have less configuration options during the creation

1.4 Set Parameters


Important Parameters:

Allow Access: <your own ip address>, <ip addresses of your BTP tenant>

<your own ip address> - it's needed for the connection from your local sql editor (DBeaver)
<ip addresses of your BTP tenant> - Otherwise Integration Suite is not able to connect to the db
https://me.sap.com/notes/0002418879 - https://help.sap.com/docs/btp/sap-business-technology-platform/regions-and-api-endpoints-available-for-cloud-foundry-environment

Public Access: Tick the checkbox

Optional:

I've unchecked Multi AZ and I've reduced the Backup Retention Period and the Memory for the tutorial (to save some money 😉). You have to check and adjust those parameters for your project.

1.5 Create the instance (Take a coffee or tea - It will take some minutes to be ready)

1.6 Create Service Key (for Authentication of Integration Suite and DBeaver)

Copy and save dbname, hostname, port, username and password for later steps

 

2. JDBC Configuration in SAP Integration Suite

2.1 Add a new resource with the credentials from the step before


JDBC URL:  jdbc:postgresql://<hostname>:<port>/dbname

 

3. Connect to PostgreSQL with (local) DBeaver

This step is needed for the creation of a table and corresponding columns that the Integration Suite is able to insert data there.

3.1 Download DBeaver or any other sql client/ editor
3.2 Connect to the db using the credentials
3.3 Create a table (e.g. employeedata)
3.4 Create columns (e.g. emp_id and emp_name)

 

 

4. Create IFlow to insert Data

4.1 Go to your Integration Suite and create a new IFlow

JDBC Receiver Adapter: Enter the JDBC Alias which you created in step 2.1

Content Modifier:

 

<root>
<Insert_Statement1>
<dbTableName action="INSERT">
<table>employeedata</table>
<access>
<emp_id>42</emp_id>
<emp_name>Brian</emp_name>
</access>
</dbTableName>
</Insert_Statement1>
</root>

 

Note: Here you can find sample batch payloads to insert multiple records: https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-integration/sap-cloud-integration/batch-payload-and-operation

4.2 Deploy it and check via DBeaver, if your data was inserted:

entry.title: Re: First steps to work with SAP Cloud ALM Deployment scenario for SAP ABAP systems (7.40 or higher)
entry.content:
Hello Neni, 

You would create transport target group between Development and Quality systems and then create another transport target group between Quality and Production systems. 

I mean create initially this transport route DEV:XXX->/TG_1/ ->QA:YYY-> /TG_2/ -> VIR:ZZZ with CTC=1, transport routes client dependent

/TG_1/ contains QA:YYY, and /TG_2/ contains initially VIR: ZZZ

Then when you get the PRD:ZZZ real system you can add PRD:ZZZ to this transport group /TG_2/ and remove VIR:ZZZ from it.

The change in the TMS transport route will add the TR already imported into QA:YYY, into the import buffer of PRD:ZZZ.

At Feature level you will have the DEVK9... TR released and imported until QA:YYY with target tenant /TG_2/ .

I hope this helps, best regards,

Dolores
